## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this lesson, you'll learn exactly how to calculate gross margin and how to work out your contribution margin after marketing. Knowing your contribution margin will help you understand what is a better buying price or how you negotiate with your suppliers. This is something I wish I knew when I was studying out my first business. So knowing your contribution margin is the key. Contribution margin is basically your gross margin taking out all the variable costs associated to making a sale. So those little costs including your merchant facility cost, you know, if you're using Shopify, you're probably using Stripe or PayPal. And that also includes your last mile delivery cost, also includes your marketing cost. And because without these costs, you wouldn't be able to fulfill an order or wouldn't be able to earn the revenue. This will definitely give you a better idea how much money you should be spending on your marketing, on your product, and how much you should price your product at. Now I'm going to show you how you calculate your profit and loss and your contribution margin in a very simple template that I created for you. I break them into two parts because the first part is cost associated to operation. The second part is cost associated to acquisition. So basically all your marketing spend. I really encourage all e-commerce business entrepreneurs and use something similar to keep a close eye on your product profit margin. This is a simpler version of the exact template I'm using for July. So let's look at column C. This is 2019 July actual numbers. So this is where you set all your benchmark. So say in July you made $4,000 in revenue and you did 10% discount throughout the month. So that's 10% of your sales. So $400 and your net revenue is $4,000 minus $400, $3,600 as your net revenue. And your merchant facility fee, you probably know this as well. So roughly about 1 to 2%. So let's just say 2% here. Your cost is basically your manufacturing cost. Let's say it's 50%, that's $2,000. Delivery cost is roughly about 10% of your sales. So your gross profit is your gross revenue minus all these costs. So it worked out to be $1,168. So let's move to marketing. So these are all the marketing costs accumulated for the month of July. So total $750.

## [03:00 - 04:52]

And you can see from your Google Analytics and you got 1,000 visitors of the month. So you'll be able to work out your blended cost per click for the month. You basically is $750 divided by the total visitors. So that's $0.75 per click. So it's very important to know all these benchmark. Okay. And the next one is how many sales you've done for the month. You've done 20 and worked out to be 2% of your conversion. So if you don't know how to calculate conversion rate, this is basically your orders, order numbers divided by the total sessions. And that's your conversion numbers. So this set out to be like a rough benchmark for the month of July. And if you look at contribution margin, and it's basically the gross profit minus your marketing cost for the month. And that's $418. And then you can work out your contribution margin percentage, which is your contribution margin divided by your gross revenue number. So it worked out to be 10.45%. I think like 10% contribution margin is a quite healthy contribution margin. In my perspective, it means that you can actually scale your business and buying a lot more stock and doing more marketing. But if your contribution margin is actually negative, it means that you shouldn't be spending too much money on marketing. You should work out the most efficient way to acquire customers, or you should probably buy at a cheaper rate from your suppliers. So the action item for this lesson is to download this PNL template and work out your contribution margin by plugging in your numbers. Do that now, and I'll see you in the next lesson.
